overview.body: This is an ENHANCE week. You'll copy your Lab 2.1 code and add powerful new features: your own reusable function library, D20-based travel, a Destination Gate coin flip, and random hazard encounters. By the end, your road trip will be unpredictable, exciting, and uniquely yours.

mentorQuote.quote: Time to level up! You've got a working travel loop, but let's be honest — driving exactly 50 miles every turn? Boring. This week, we're adding randomness. You'll create your own Python module with dice rolls and coin flips, then watch Texas throw curveballs at your carefully planned road trip.

objectives: Create function_library.py with three reusable functions: flip_coin(), roll_d20(), and check_percentage()
Import and use your own module in lab-2-2.py
Implement the Destination Gate — a coin flip that decides if Texas lets you go where you want
Replace fixed 50-mile travel with D20 rolls (roll × 10 = miles per turn)
Add hazard encounters with a 30% chance per turn
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 5: Functions
programRequirements: Your lab-2-2.py MUST import and use your function_library module
Your program MUST implement the Destination Gate using flip_coin()
Your program MUST use D20 rolls for travel distance instead of fixed 50 miles
Your program MUST implement hazard encounters with 30% chance per turn
overview.filenameNote: Submit two files this week: function_library.py (your reusable module) and lab-2-2.py (your main game).
overview.additionalFilesNote: CodeGrade tests your functions in isolation, so both files must be present in your submission.
overview.saveYourCode: Keep both files — Lab 2.3 copies both and adds input validation functions to your library. Your toolkit keeps growing!

rows.task: Replace fixed 50 miles with dice rolls
Random events with coin flip outcomes

timeGuide.strategy: Work in two coding sessions if needed. Checkpoints 1–3 create your library and set up the game. Checkpoints 4–6 add the fun mechanics. Each session is about 35–45 minutes.

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here's how your finished program fits together. You're starting from Lab 2.1 — the [NEW] and [REPLACED] tags show what changes this week.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- function_library.py (NEW FILE) ----
import random

def flip_coin():             returns 'HEADS' or 'TAILS'  [NEW-CP1]
def roll_d20():              returns int 1-20             [NEW-CP2]
def check_percentage(chance): returns True/False          [NEW-CP2]

# ---- lab-2-2.py ----
import random
import function_library                                    [NEW-CP3]

# CONSTANTS
SAT_TO_CORPUS = 150
SAT_TO_HOUSTON = 200
SAT_TO_AUSTIN = 80
MILES_MULTIPLIER = 10                                     [NEW-CP3]
HAZARD_CHANCE = 30                                        [NEW-CP3]

def main():
│
│── Welcome banner, player name
│── 3-choice menu → set destination, distance
│
│── DESTINATION GATE:                                     [NEW-CP4]
│   flip = function_library.flip_coin()
│   if HEADS: keep destination
│   else: redirect to random other destination
│
│── distance_remaining, total_miles, turn = ...
│── total_hazards = 0, total_escaped = 0                  [NEW-CP6]
│
│   TRAVEL LOOP:
│   while distance_remaining > 0:
│   │── roll = function_library.roll_d20()                [NEW-CP5]
│   │── miles_this_turn = roll * MILES_MULTIPLIER         [REPLACED-CP5]
│   │── Display turn, roll, miles
│   │── Update distance_remaining, total_miles, turn
│   │── HAZARD CHECK:                                     [NEW-CP6]
│   │   if function_library.check_percentage(HAZARD_CHANCE):
│   │       pick random hazard 1-8
│   │       flip coin for outcome
│   │       update hazard counters
│
│── Arrival + TRIP SUMMARY (with hazard stats)            [REPLACED-CP6]

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

content.body: A brand new file called function_library.py containing your first custom function: flip_coin(). This module will grow throughout the semester — you're building your own developer toolkit!

Real developers don't write the same code over and over. They create reusable modules they can import into any project. When you type import function_library, you're doing exactly what professionals do with import random or import math.

items: Create a new file named exactly function_library.py in the same folder as your main game
Add a module docstring at the very top with your name, a description like "Bat City Collective — Your Personal Developer Toolkit"
Add import random after the docstring
Define a function called flip_coin() with no parameters and a docstring explaining what it returns
Inside flip_coin(): use random.randint(0, 1) to get 0 or 1, then use if/else to return the string 'HEADS' for 1 or 'TAILS' for 0

references.description: You used random.randint(MIN, MAX) to simulate dice. Same pattern here — but return a string instead of printing.
Shows how functions return values using the return statement. Your flip_coin() uses the same pattern.

content.body: Add this temporary test code at the bottom of function_library.py:

content.code:
# DEBUG - Delete after testing
print('Testing flip_coin():')
for i in range(10):
    print(f'  Flip {i + 1}: {flip_coin()}')

content.body: Run function_library.py directly. Your results will vary (it's random!), but you should see a mix of HEADS and TAILS:

content.output:
Testing flip_coin():
  Flip 1: HEADS
  Flip 2: TAILS
  Flip 3: HEADS
  Flip 4: HEADS
  Flip 5: TAILS
  Flip 6: TAILS
  Flip 7: HEADS
  Flip 8: TAILS
  Flip 9: HEADS
  Flip 10: TAILS

content.title: Delete the test code

rows: Putting import random inside the function
Works but inefficient — imports should be at the top of the file
print() shows output but doesn't give the value back to calling code
Forgetting the colon after def flip_coin()
SyntaxError — function definitions need that colon!

items: function_library.py with docstring, import, and flip_coin()
Function returns 'HEADS' or 'TAILS' randomly
Test code deleted

content.body: Two more functions: roll_d20() simulates a 20-sided die, and check_percentage(chance) determines if random events happen.

items: In function_library.py, define roll_d20() with no parameters and a docstring saying it returns an integer from 1 to 20
Have it return random.randint(1, 20)
Define check_percentage(chance) with one parameter called chance and a docstring explaining the parameter and return value
Inside: roll random.randint(1, 100) and return True if the roll is less than or equal to chance, otherwise False

references.description: Shows how to pass data into a function using parameters. Your check_percentage(chance) uses the same pattern — chance is a parameter just like cups was.

content.body: Add this temporary test code at the bottom of function_library.py:
Run and verify D20 rolls are 1–20, and 30% hits roughly 4–8 times out of 20:

content.output:
Testing roll_d20():
  Roll 1: 14
  Roll 2: 3
  Roll 3: 19
  Roll 4: 7
  Roll 5: 11
Testing check_percentage(30):
  30% triggered 5 times out of 20

content.title: Delete all test code
content.body: Delete all test code before moving to Checkpoint 3.

rows: D20 rolls are 1–20, not 0–20 — use randint(1, 20)
Comparing roll to chance with < instead of <=
30% chance means roll 1–30 hits, which needs <= 30
Functions need docstrings explaining what they do and return

items: function_library.py with three working functions, each with a docstring
flip_coin() returns 'HEADS' or 'TAILS'
roll_d20() returns integer 1–20
check_percentage(chance) returns True or False

content.body: Your lab-2-2.py file with updated imports, docstring, and new constants. The game should still work exactly like 2.1 after this checkpoint.

items: Copy your lab-2-1.py file and rename it lab-2-2.py
Update the docstring: change title to "Lab 2.2: Texas Has Other Plans" and update the description
Add import random at the top (after docstring)
Add import function_library right after — this imports YOUR module!
In your constants section, add: MILES_MULTIPLIER = 10
Add another constant: HAZARD_CHANCE = 30

references.description: You imported the math module. Same pattern: import function_library. Then call functions with function_library.flip_coin().

content.quote: After copying code from a previous assignment, always run it BEFORE adding new features. We call this regression testing — making sure the old stuff still works. If the copied code is broken, you'll waste hours debugging new code when the bug was already there!

content.body: Add this debug code right after your constants in main():

content.code:
# DEBUG - Delete after testing
    print('Testing imports...')
    print(f'Coin flip: {function_library.flip_coin()}')
    print(f'D20 roll: {function_library.roll_d20()}')
    print()

content.body: Run lab-2-2.py — you should see the test output, then the game runs normally:

content.title: Delete the debug code, then regression test
content.body: Delete the DEBUG code, then verify the game still works exactly like Lab 2.1 before moving to Checkpoint 4.

rows: lab-2-2.py and function_library.py must be in the same directory

items: lab-2-2.py with updated docstring
Both import random and import function_library at the top
New constants: MILES_MULTIPLIER and HAZARD_CHANCE
Game runs and works like Lab 2.1

content.body: After the player picks a destination, flip a coin. HEADS = you get your choice. TAILS = Texas redirects you to a random different destination!

items: Find where you set destination and distance based on player choice
Store the player's original choice: player_choice = choice
After your if-elif-else, print a dramatic message and call function_library.flip_coin()
If 'HEADS': print success message, keep destination as-is
If 'TAILS': print "Texas has other plans...", use random.randint() to pick one of the OTHER two destinations
Update destination and distance if redirected

content.body: Run the game several times, always picking the same destination. You should see both outcomes:

content.output:
HEADS! Texas lets you through!
You're headed to Houston (200 miles)

--- or ---

TAILS! Texas has other plans...
You're being redirected to Corpus Christi (150 miles)

rows: Your function returns 'HEADS' (all caps) — case must match!
TAILS redirect means the destination AND distance both change
Putting gate logic in wrong place
Gate happens AFTER player chooses but BEFORE travel begins

items: Coin flip happens after destination choice
HEADS keeps the original destination
TAILS redirects to a different destination (both destination and distance update)
Game continues with the final destination

content.body: Instead of traveling exactly 50 miles per turn, roll a D20 × 10. Roll a 15? Travel 150 miles! Roll a 3? Only 30 miles.

items: Find where you calculate miles traveled inside your while loop
Replace the fixed 50 with a call to function_library.roll_d20() — store the result as roll
Calculate miles_this_turn = roll * MILES_MULTIPLIER
Display the roll and calculated miles to the player
Update your distance subtraction and total_miles accumulator using miles_this_turn

content.body: Run and verify miles vary each turn:

content.output:
--- Turn 1 ---
Rolling D20... You rolled 15!
You travel 150 miles. 50 miles remaining.

--- Turn 2 ---
Rolling D20... You rolled 8!
You travel 80 miles.
You made it to Houston!

rows: Infinite loop — not subtracting from remaining
Loop condition never becomes false if you don't update distance_remaining
Use > 0 — you might overshoot exact zero with variable rolls
Turn numbers stay the same if you forget turn = turn + 1

items: D20 roll each turn (1–20)
Miles traveled varies (10–200 per turn)
Roll displayed to player each turn
Trip summary shows actual total miles

content.body: 30% chance per turn of hitting a hazard. When one triggers, flip a coin: HEADS = escape, TAILS = face the consequence.

items: Before your while loop, add: total_hazards = 0 and total_escaped = 0
At the END of each loop iteration (after travel), call function_library.check_percentage(HAZARD_CHANCE)
If it returns True: a hazard occurs — generate a random hazard number (1–8) using random.randint(1, 8)
Use if-elif-else to display the hazard name from the reference table below
Flip a coin: HEADS = print escape message, TAILS = print consequence message
Increment total_hazards every time a hazard fires, increment total_escaped only on HEADS
Add hazard stats to your Trip Summary: Hazards Faced and Hazards Escaped

content.body: Texas Hazard Reference:

rows: Small town officer, lost an hour

content.body: Run multiple times until you see hazards trigger:

content.output:
HAZARD! Buc-ee's appears!
Flipping for your fate... TAILS!
Lost 2 hours on beef jerky.

==================================================
TRIP SUMMARY
==================================================
Hazards Faced: 2
Hazards Escaped: 1

rows: Hazard check outside the travel loop
Hazards should happen each turn while traveling, not just once
Track total_hazards (every hazard) AND total_escaped (HEADS only)
Use randint(1, 8) to pick a random hazard each time

items: 30% hazard chance using check_percentage() inside the travel loop
8 different hazards with HEADS/TAILS outcomes
total_hazards and total_escaped tracking variables
Trip summary shows hazard stats

beforeYouSubmit: lab-2-2.py has updated docstring with your name, abc123, section, and description
Destination Gate coin flip happens after choice but before travel
Hazard encounters check inside the travel loop
No DEBUG code in either file
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapters 2–5 only. CodeGrade will flag code using techniques from later chapters.

specific: All previous techniques (variables, if/else, while loops)
Functions with parameters and return values
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: lists, dictionaries, file I/O, or any Chapter 6+ techniques

finalChecklist.saveYourCode: Keep both files — Lab 2.3 copies both and adds input validation functions to your library. Your toolkit keeps growing!

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read Program 5-24 (sale_price.py) and Program 5-21 (dice.py) — the return value and random patterns are exactly what you need
Test your library separately before using it in the main game — run function_library.py directly
Add DEBUG prints to see what your functions return
Submit to CodeGrade early — it tests function_library.py in isolation, so you'll know if a function is broken
Come to Zoom or office hours — we can screen share and debug together
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 5 BookEx Reference Guide — step-by-step breakdowns of every textbook example, your primary reference
